definition: A promise by one party to compensate the other for losses or damages arising from the contract or specific events.
Legal responsibility for one's acts or omissions. In contracts, this determines who bears financial risk.
A clause that frees both parties from obligation when an extraordinary event beyond their control prevents performance (e.g., natural disaster, war, pandemic).
Violation of a contractual obligation by failing to perform, by making performance impossible, or by repudiating the contract.
A method of dispute resolution where parties agree to submit their dispute to a neutral third party (arbitrator) instead of going to court.
A voluntary dispute resolution process where a neutral mediator helps parties reach a mutually acceptable agreement.
A restriction preventing a party from engaging in a competing business or profession for a specified time and geographic area after the contract ends.
A clause that prohibits a party from soliciting or hiring the other party's employees, customers, or clients.
An obligation to keep certain information private and not disclose it to third parties.
A legally binding contract establishing a confidential relationship between parties who agree to protect sensitive information.
Creations of the mind (inventions, designs, names, images) used in commerce, protected by patents, copyrights, and trademarks.
The clause specifying which jurisdiction's laws will be used to interpret and enforce the contract.
The authority of a court to hear and decide a case. Contract clauses often specify which court has jurisdiction.
Something of value exchanged between parties that makes a contract legally binding (money, services, goods, promises).
The maximum time period after an event within which legal proceedings may be initiated.
The voluntary relinquishment of a known right. A party may waive certain contractual rights.
A clause stating that if one part of a contract is found invalid or unenforceable, the remaining parts still apply.
The transfer of contractual rights or obligations from one party to another.
Specifies the conditions under which the contract can be ended before its natural expiration.
A predetermined amount of money that must be paid as damages for breach of contract.
Indirect damages that result from a breach of contract, not from the breach itself but from its consequences.
A promise or guarantee that a statement of fact is true, or that a product or service will meet certain standards.
A statement of fact made by one party to induce the other to enter into the contract.
A significant violation of contract terms that permits the aggrieved party to terminate the contract and seek damages.
A specified amount of time given to a breaching party to fix (cure) their breach before the other party can terminate.
States that the written contract represents the complete agreement between parties, superseding prior negotiations.
A formal change or addition to the contract terms, usually requiring written agreement of all parties.
An implied obligation to deal honestly and fairly, without intent to defraud or seek unconscionable advantage.
A contract term so unfair that no reasonable person would agree to it. Courts may refuse to enforce such terms.
A court order requiring a party to perform their contractual obligations rather than pay damages.
The cancellation of a contract, returning parties to their pre-contract positions.
A legal principle preventing a party from asserting something contrary to what is implied by their previous actions or statements.
The relationship between the parties to a contract. Generally, only parties to a contract can enforce it.
A response to an offer that changes the terms, effectively rejecting the original offer and creating a new one.
The legal ability of a party to enter into a contract. Minors and mentally incapacitated persons may lack capacity.
When a party is forced to enter a contract through threats or coercion, making the contract potentially voidable.
Improper use of power or trust to persuade a party to enter a contract against their free will.
A false statement of fact made to induce a party to enter into a contract.
Replacing an existing contract with a new one, or substituting a new party for an original party.
A contract between a party to an original contract and a third party to perform some or all of the original obligations.
A legal claim on property as security for the payment of a debt or obligation.
A financial arrangement where a third party holds funds until specified conditions are met.
Monetary compensation awarded for loss or injury caused by breach of contract or other legal wrong.
A court order requiring a party to do or refrain from doing a specific act.
A law requiring certain types of contracts (e.g., real estate, contracts over one year) to be in writing to be enforceable.
A rule that prevents parties from introducing extrinsic evidence of prior or contemporaneous agreements to contradict the written contract.
An event or action that must occur before a party's obligation under a contract becomes effective.
An event that, if it occurs, terminates an existing obligation under a contract.
A person who benefits from a contract to which they are not a direct party.
A binding promise in a contract. Affirmative covenants require action; negative covenants restrict action.
Introductory statements in a contract that provide context, identify parties, and state the purpose of the agreement.
Introductory clauses in a contract that explain the background and intent of the agreement.
A legal concept where parties agree to accept different performance than originally required to settle a dispute.
When one party benefits at the expense of another without legal justification, creating an obligation to make restitution.
A court-imposed obligation to prevent unjust enrichment when no actual contract exists between parties.
A doctrine preventing a party from going back on a promise when the other party relied on it to their detriment.
A standardized contract drafted by the party with superior bargaining power, offered on a take-it-or-leave-it basis.
A situation where buyer and seller exchange standard forms with conflicting terms, raising questions about which terms govern.
Standard contract language that is commonly used across many contracts, often found at the end.
A contractual provision specifying which court or tribunal will resolve disputes.
Mixing of funds from different sources, which can be problematic in trust and fiduciary relationships.
A legal fiction that a person is presumed to have knowledge of something because it is publicly available or recorded.
Too trivial or minor to merit consideration. Courts may not enforce claims for de minimis breaches.
Non-monetary remedies ordered by a court, such as injunctions or specific performance.
The highest standard of care, requiring a party to act in the best interest of another party.
A clause where one party agrees not to hold the other responsible for certain damages or losses.
An unwritten guarantee that a product or service will meet a minimum level of quality.
A defense asserting that both parties are equally at fault, potentially barring either from recovery.
Each party is independently liable for the full extent of the obligations, regardless of their individual share.
A cap on the amount one party can recover from the other for damages arising from the contract.
The obligation of an injured party to take reasonable steps to minimize their losses after a breach.
Failure to exercise the care that a reasonably prudent person would exercise in similar circumstances.
A clear proposal to enter into a contract on specified terms, made with the intention of being bound upon acceptance.
Unqualified agreement to the terms of an offer, creating a binding contract.
An agreement where one party pays for the right, but not the obligation, to buy or sell something at a set price within a specified time.
A contractual provision imposing an excessive payment for breach. Courts may refuse to enforce true penalties.
A legal document authorizing one person to act on behalf of another in legal or financial matters.
A legal principle established by a court decision that guides future decisions in similar cases.
Proportional allocation, often used in calculating shared costs, damages, or refunds.
A claim for the reasonable value of services rendered when there is no enforceable contract.
Formal approval or confirmation of an act that was previously unauthorized.
The right to demand payment or compensation, or the act of seeking such remedies.
Returning something to its rightful owner or compensating for loss to restore the injured party.
A clause allowing the seller to retain ownership of goods until full payment is received.
A contractual right giving a party the first opportunity to make an offer before the other party can negotiate with third parties.
The principle that a government cannot be sued without its consent.
The right of one party to step into the shoes of another to pursue a claim.
An unconditional offer of performance by one party, coupled with the ability to perform.
A clause making timely performance a condition of the contract, so any delay constitutes a material breach.
When a third party intentionally disrupts a contractual relationship between two other parties.
An act performed beyond the legal power or authority of the person or entity performing it.
A contract that may be affirmed or rejected at the option of one party due to a defect (e.g., fraud, duress, minority).
A contract that has no legal force from the beginning, as if it never existed (e.g., contract for illegal activity).
A guarantee that the seller has the legal right to transfer ownership of the goods.
Materials prepared by or for an attorney in anticipation of litigation, generally protected from disclosure.
The maximum amount a party will pay in indemnification claims under the contract.
Specifies which contract provisions remain in effect after the contract terminates or expires.
A clause guaranteeing a party the best terms offered to any other party in similar agreements.
Prohibits or restricts a party from transferring their rights or obligations to a third party without consent.
A clause that extends the contract term automatically unless one party provides notice of non-renewal.
A provision triggered when ownership of one party changes, often allowing the other party to terminate.
Rights allowing majority shareholders to force minority shareholders to join in the sale of a company.
Rights protecting minority shareholders by allowing them to join when majority shareholders sell their stake.
A contractual provision where the seller receives additional payment based on the business achieving certain post-acquisition targets.
A neutral third party holding funds, documents, or assets until contract conditions are satisfied.
Restricts a party from entering into similar agreements with competitors during the contract term.
